title: SEGMENT 18 — The lasting legacy: the $100 genome, polygenic risk scores, AI-genomics integration

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The lasting legacy: the $100 genome, polygenic risk scores, AI-genomics integration
We end on legacy and on where the field is going.
Five threads I want to leave you with.
Thread one: the cost curve, and the $100 genome.
In 1990, sequencing a human genome cost $3 billion.
In 2003, the second human genome would have cost about $300 million.
By 2008, the first individual personal genomes were costing about $1 million each.
In 2014, the cost crossed the $1,000 genome threshold, the symbolic target Illumina had been chasing for years.
Today in 2026, a clinical whole genome runs about $200, and research-grade genomes from major sequencing centers can be produced for well under that.
The $100 genome is essentially here.
The next target, often discussed, is the $10 genome — at which point sequencing becomes cheap enough to be a routine clinical commodity, like a blood test.
Thread two: polygenic risk scores.
We talked in the previous segment about how common disease is polygenic.

The clinical translation of that finding is the polygenic risk score, PRS — a single number computed from a person's genotype across hundreds or thousands of disease-associated variants, weighted by their effect sizes from GWAS.

For some diseases — coronary artery disease, breast cancer, type 2 diabetes — polygenic risk scores can now identify the top few percent of the population as having genetic risk equivalent to a strong monogenic mutation.
PRS is starting to be used clinically for risk stratification, screening prioritization, and prevention.
The accuracy and equity issues are still being worked out, but PRS is the most credible operationalization of the personalized medicine vision that HGP originally promised.
Thread three: biobank-scale science.
We covered this in Segment 15.
The unit of analysis is now a million genomes, linked to clinical records, imaging, wearable data, environmental exposures.
This is a fundamentally different kind of human genetics than what existed before 2010, and it is producing findings on a scale that simply was not possible in the original HGP era.
Thread four: AI-genomics integration.
The most exciting and rapidly developing frontier in genomics right now is the integration of machine learning with genomic data.

Deep learning models trained on genomic sequence can now predict, with substantial accuracy, which variants will affect splicing, which regions of the genome are likely to be regulatory, which non-coding variants are likely to disrupt enhancer function.
Protein structure prediction — AlphaFold and its successors — has solved the problem of going from amino acid sequence to three-dimensional structure for nearly every known protein.
Foundation models trained on biological sequence are starting to generalize across tasks in ways that resemble what large language models do for text.

The intersection of AI and genomics is where a lot of the next decade's progress will happen, and that intersection is only possible because HGP and its successors produced the foundational datasets that the AI models need.
Thread five: equity and global genomics.
The greatest open issue in genomics right now is equity.
The vast majority of genomic data, the reference itself, the variant catalogues, the GWAS samples, the biobank cohorts — have been heavily biased toward European-ancestry samples.

This has direct clinical consequences: variant interpretation is more accurate for European patients than for everyone else, polygenic risk scores trained in European cohorts perform substantially worse when applied to other populations, and the benefits of genomic medicine have been unequally distributed.
The Human Pangenome Consortium, the All of Us program, the African and Latin American biobank initiatives, the H3Africa consortium, and several other efforts are working to correct this imbalance.
The next decade of genomics will be defined in part by how successfully the field shifts from a European-centric to a globally representative dataset.
So where does that leave us, looking back on HGP from 2026?

The Human Genome Project was biology's moon landing — and like the moon landing, its most important legacy is not the artifact itself but the infrastructure, the techniques, the culture, and the political and institutional precedents it left behind.
The 2003 reference sequence has been superseded by T2T-CHM13 and by the Human Pangenome.
The original twenty-thousand-gene catalogue has been refined and re-annotated thousands of times.

The technologies of 1990 — Sanger sequencing, BAC cloning, gel electrophoresis — are essentially obsolete, replaced by short-read sequencing platforms, then long-read platforms, and now whatever comes next.
But the open-data culture established by the Bermuda Principles is still the default in genomics.
The ELSI framework is still the model for thinking about emerging biotechnology ethics.
The pattern of large, internationally coordinated, publicly funded biology — once thought to be impossible, then thought to be a one-off — is now routine.
The cost curve of sequencing has dropped by seven orders of magnitude.

Genomic medicine, while it has not delivered everything that was promised, has delivered real things — Mendelian disease diagnosis, precision oncology, pharmacogenomics, polygenic risk stratification — that did not exist before HGP.
If you walk out of here remembering one thing, remember the moon landing analogy.
The audacious goal, the decade-long collaborative effort, the political commitment, the spinoff technologies that mattered more than the original artifact.
That is what HGP was, and that is why it is the foundational story of modern biology.
Thank you.
